quote: <Ref k="nakagawa2024" p="41" />; <Ref k="sato2008" p="§2.3" />; <Ref k="shiraishi2022" p="§4.1" />.
issue: The citation for Sato 2008 uses a section number (§2.3) while others use page numbers; this may reflect inconsistent locator format for the same type of source.
suggestion: Standardize locator format—either use page numbers for all or clarify if section numbers are intentional.

quote: Nakagawa (2024: 25) characterizes Hokkaido Ainu as having relatively few phonemes and a straightforward syllable structure, noting that the five-vowel inventory is constant across all Hokkaido dialects — dialect differences are comparatively small.
issue: The em dash creates a run-on sentence; it would be clearer as two sentences or with a semicolon.
suggestion: Split into two sentences: '...all Hokkaido dialects. Dialect differences are comparatively small.'

quote: (Nakagawa 2024: 43.) The algorithm treats /y/ and /w/ as consonants, so
issue: There is a period after the citation in parentheses, creating a double punctuation mark before the next sentence.
suggestion: Remove the period after the citation: '(Nakagawa 2024: 43) The algorithm...'

quote: <Ex m="mat-ikor" g="woman-treasure" ain="macikor" tr="women's possessions" note="The underlying boundary mat|ikor resyllabifies to ma.ci.kor [ma.ʨi.kor] (CV.CV.CVC). The coda /t/ shifts to onset position and undergoes the obligatory t+i → [ʨi] palatalization. The same surface form is attested throughout the Saru corpus, e.g. usa macikor usa tamasay 'various women's treasures and bead-necklaces'." cite="nakagawa2024:36" dial="SAR" />
issue: The gloss 'woman-treasure' does not match the free translation 'women's possessions' in number (singular vs plural) and word sense (compound vs possessive).
suggestion: Revise gloss to 'women-treasure' or 'woman-possession' to align with translation.

issue: In the table under 'Permitted coda consonants', the phoneme /c/ is referred to in prose as /c/ [ʨ], but the table uses /c/ without consistent bracketing of the IPA equivalent. Also, the example for /s/ includes cis, which may be better spelled cís to reflect pitch accent, though not strictly required.
suggestion: Ensure consistent notation: either always include [ʨ] after /c/ in prose, or standardize representation. Orthographic consistency for cis is acceptable if unaccented, but verify against source.

issue: A Discord attribution is tagged with ‹corpus-confirmed›, which implies broader corpus validation, but a single user report does not meet that standard.
suggestion: Change evidence grade to ‹speculative› or ‹community-observation› until corroborated.

issue: Discord citation used for a claim about near-absence of /-ow/, tagged with ‹corpus-suggested›, but a single user report cannot support a negative frequency claim without broader data.
suggestion: Reclassify as ‹speculative› or add qualifier like 'preliminary observation'.

issue: Same issue as line 352: Discord user cited for a near-zero frequency claim about /-m/, tagged ‹corpus-suggested›, which overstates evidential support.
suggestion: Revise evidence grade to ‹speculative› or note as preliminary.

issue: Discord citation supports a generalization about epenthetic vowel choice; tagging as ‹corpus-suggested› implies broader data than a single user provides.
suggestion: Change to ‹speculative› or attribute more cautiously.

quote: Among the starred onset+vowel combinations, /-c/ and /-h/ are also categorically excluded from coda position (see the preceding section), and the /-c/ exclusion from coda means that *-ac, *-ec, etc., are all absent.
issue: The text says /-c/ and /-h/ are excluded from coda position, but earlier in section 'Permitted coda consonants' it correctly states only /c/ and /h/ are excluded. However, this sentence implies both are onset exclusions, which is incorrect. The confusion arises from ambiguous phrasing: 'starred onset+vowel combinations' refers to syllable onsets, but /-c/ and /-h/ are coda exclusions.
suggestion: Rephrase to clarify: 'Although /c/ and /h/ are excluded from coda position, the absence of *-ac, *-ec, etc., follows from the coda restriction on /c/, not from onset constraints.'
